narrative_blocks.header: The firm's failure drove him from Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: As a partner in Hooker and Chaffee, he helped provision the Continental Army before French and Spanish spoliations brought the firm down after the war. He removed to Sackett's Harbor, New York, and later died at Herkimer; his wife Elizabeth survived him by just six months.

institutions.institution_blurb: Partner in the firm; dispersed to western New York after its failure.

narrative_blocks.header: The firm's failure drove him from Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: As a partner in Hooker and Chaffee, he helped provision the Continental Army before French and Spanish spoliations brought the firm down after the war. He removed to Sackett's Harbor, New York, and later died at Herkimer; his wife Elizabeth survived him by just six months.

institutions.institution_blurb: Partner in the firm; dispersed to western New York after its failure.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-seven; two young children survived her
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Hooker married Richard Lord Jones in Hartford in 1793 and died on May 18, 1800 in Granville, New York, at twenty-seven — leaving behind two children, Elizabeth (born August 1792) and Horace (born January 1795), who were seven and five years old when she died. Her husband Richard Lord Jones outlived her by fifty-two years, dying in Indiana in 1852 at eighty-five; their daughter Elizabeth lived to eighty-nine, outliving her mother by eighty-two years.

burial.plot_notes: Old Yard, Middle Granville, Washington County NY per FA

bio.photo_notes: Richard Lord Jones (1767-1852); photo attribution: see research_notes re filename discrepancy

burial.plot_notes: Fairview Cemetery New Albany Indiana; stone reads 'A musician in the Army of the Revolution'; newer government stone reads 'Ten Year Old Fifer'

career.notes: Engaged in cotton manufacturing business in Connecticut after the war. EH describes him as 'a merchant at Hartford.'

career.organization: Gallipolis, Ohio; later New Albany, Indiana
career.notes: Relocated to Gallipolis Ohio in 1818, then to a farm near New Albany Indiana where he died in 1852.

military_service.unit: Captain James Watson's Company, Third Connecticut Regiment (Colonel Samuel B. Webb)
military_service.notes: Enlisted June 20, 1777 at age 10 — one of the youngest soldiers in the Revolutionary War. Served as fifer; participated in each march and engagement of his unit. Met both George and Martha Washington and General Lafayette. Served full 3-year term; discharged June 20, 1780. Gravestone: 'A musician in the Army of the Revolution' / 'Ten Year Old Fifer'.

narrative_blocks.header: His tombstone reads: 'Ten Year Old Fifer'
narrative_blocks.body: Richard Lord Jones enlisted in June 1777 in Colonel Samuel B. Webb's Regiment and was put under the Master of the Band to learn fife; arriving at Morristown in January 1780, he would later write: 'The Inhabitants remarked that it was the coldest winter ever known by the oldest inhabitants. The troops drew no rations of meat for two days (hungry times.)' His gravestone at Fairview Cemetery in New Albany, Indiana carries two inscriptions — the original reads 'A musician in the Army of the Revolution,' and a government-issued stone beside it reads 'Ten Year Old Fifer,' for a man who was ten when he shouldered a fife into the Continental Army.

narrative_blocks.header: He sang God Save America for Martha Washington
narrative_blocks.body: Colonel Webb directed Jones to go to Colonel Jackson's hut, where Martha Washington and some ladies were, and sing her a song; Jones later wrote: 'I did so sing the song when she presented me with a bill of three dollars, Continental Currency. The bill has been kept, folded as she gave it to me.' The three-dollar Continental bill — kept folded precisely as Martha Washington handed it to a ten-year-old fifer in a New Jersey officer's hut in 1780 — is one of the most extraordinary artifacts in this entire family's history, passed from Washington's hands to a child soldier who would live to farm in Indiana and die at eighty-five in 1852.

narrative_blocks.header: Walked 150 miles home to Hartford at thirteen
narrative_blocks.body: When Jones received his discharge on June 20, 1780 — having served his full three-year term from age ten to thirteen — he left Jockey Hollow with two other discharged men and walked 150 miles north to Hartford, Connecticut, recording in his memoir that he arrived home 'without any accident.' That phrase — 'without any accident,' after a 150-mile walk home from a war entered at ten — is a small window into what it meant to be a child soldier in the Continental Army, and into the plainspoken Yankee temperament with which Jones recorded his entire service in the memoir he would write decades later in Indiana.

burial.plot_notes: Lakeside Cemetery Sackets Harbor Jefferson County NY per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Born before the Hartford wedding; lived ninety years
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hooker Jones was born August 14, 1792 — a year before her parents Richard Lord Jones and Hannah Hooker were recorded as marrying in Hartford in 1793, a discrepancy in the records that may reflect an earlier ceremony, an error in the marriage date, or a gap in the documentary record. Her mother Hannah died in May 1800 when Elizabeth was seven; Elizabeth lived to eighty-nine, dying February 4, 1882 in Sackets Harbor, New York — outliving her mother by eighty-two years and her brother Horace Hooker Jones by thirty-one.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

burial.plot_notes: Cave Hill Cemetery Louisville KY per FA; burial delayed to Feb 17, 1852

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' preserves his mother Hannah Hooker's birth family in the Jones line — she died in 1800 when he was approximately five years old.

narrative_blocks.header: His mother died before he turned six
narrative_blocks.body: Horace Hooker Jones was born January 6, 1795 with 'Hooker' as his middle name, carrying his mother Hannah Hooker's birth family forward into the Jones line. His mother died in May 1800 when he was five; he died in December 1851 at fifty-six in Louisville, Kentucky — a businessman and member of the firm Jones and Halbert — his burial delayed until February 17, 1852 while his widow Flora located a site at Cave Hill Cemetery.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

cross_connections.display_label: Both Hooker descendants died in Waterville Lucas County Ohio
Both families joined through Talcott siblings.
His wife's brother Matthew also married into the Hooker family line.
Both tied to separate Talcott branches in the same family generation.
Both families joined through Talcott siblings a generation later.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Governor Talcott's daughter Eunice
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice Talcott was the second daughter of Joseph Talcott, Governor of Connecticut from 1725 to 1741. Their marriage directly joined the two founding Hartford families — reinforced when Eunice's brother Matthew simultaneously married Mary Russell, herself a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Named co-heir in the Governor's estate
narrative_blocks.body: On December 25, 1742, Nathaniel and Eunice appeared before the Court of Probate in Hartford to sign Governor Joseph Talcott's estate distribution as co-heirs. It was one of the final official acts binding the Hooker family to the colonial governor's wealth.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale man, militia captain, Assembly representative
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale College in 1729 and held the rank of Captain in the Connecticut Militia as early as 1734. He served as Hartford's representative in the General Assembly across three sessions, while running a substantial Hartford mercantile estate.

narrative_blocks.header: Son James partnered in Hooker & Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: His son James Hooker was a partner in the mercantile firm Hooker & Chaffee, which grew very successful during the Revolutionary War era. The firm continued the family's Hartford commercial standing into the next generation.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law — Mary Russell married her brother Matthew Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: She and brother Matthew both married Hooker descendants
narrative_blocks.body: She married Captain Nathaniel Hooker — a direct Hooker-surname descendant. Her brother Matthew Talcott simultaneously married Mary Russell, herself a Hooker descendant through the Pierpont line. Two of Governor Joseph Talcott's children marrying into the Hooker family in the same generation is why "Talcott" became a permanent name in the Hooker tree.

cross_connections.display_label: His daughter married her son, founding lines merged.

narrative_blocks.header: His bride had been promised to his brother
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Standley had been betrothed to Nathaniel's brother Roger Hooker, who died April 29, 1698. Seven months later — December 28, 1698 — Nathaniel married her instead.

narrative_blocks.header: His wedding gift was half of Main Street
narrative_blocks.body: When Nathaniel married, his father-in-law Hon. Nathaniel Stanley gave him half his Hartford lot — extending from the Second Burial Ground to the little river. This became his place of business on the main street, just below the present Centre Church.

narrative_blocks.header: Two different Stanley families both married Hooker brothers
narrative_blocks.body: His brother John had married Abigail Standley of one Stanley family; Nathaniel married Mary Standley of a completely separate Stanley branch — Hon. Nathaniel Stanley of Hartford's line. Two unrelated families sharing the same name both converged on the Hooker brothers in the same generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Hartford's Colonial Assembly representative, dead at 40
narrative_blocks.body: He represented Hartford in the Connecticut Colonial Assembly for several years. He died November 21, 1711, at age 40, still in office.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter married into the Talcott-Edwards line
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Sarah Hooker married Hon. Daniel Edwards in 1728 — whose mother was Mary Talcott, daughter of Lt.-Col. John Talcott. Their children thus descend from both Rev. Thomas Hooker and the founding Talcott line.

bio.bio_blurb: Hartford colonial assemblyman; grandson of Thomas Hooker

cross_connections.display_label: Both Standley women married Hooker brothers — different families.

narrative_blocks.header: Half a Hartford house lot came with marriage
narrative_blocks.body: When Mary Stanley married the Hartford merchant Nathaniel Hooker in 1698, her father gave the couple half his house lot, which ran from the Center Church burying-yard down to the Little River. Nathaniel served in the legislature until his death in 1711, after which she married another noted Hartford merchant, John Austin.

political_offices.title: Governor
political_offices.notes: First Governor of Connecticut born within its limits. Longest-serving after Gov. Winthrop. Died in office Oct 11, 1741.

cross_connections.display_label: Son-in-law signed his estate — 'Nathaniel Hooker and Eunice Hooker'
His granddaughter Esther married Rev. Noadiah Russell in 1758
was the Deputy Governor whose death opened his path

notable.notable_blurb: First Connecticut governor born within its limits

narrative_blocks.header: First Connecticut governor born within the colony
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Hartford on November 16, 1669, Joseph Talcott was the first Governor of Connecticut born within the colony rather than in England. He served from 1724 until his death on October 11, 1741 — 17 years in office, longer than any predecessor except Governor Winthrop and any successor until the modern era. His funeral expenses of £69 12s. 6d. were paid from the Colonial Treasury.

narrative_blocks.header: He presided in the upper house the afternoon
narrative_blocks.body: When his wife Eunice died suddenly about noon on Thursday, May 25, 1738, the Deputy Governor was absent at Norwich. Governor Talcott did not let his private grief interfere with his official duty — he returned and presided in the upper house that same afternoon. The General Assembly appointed a committee to draw an address of condolence to the Governor.

narrative_blocks.header: His estate was signed by 'Nathaniel Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: At the Court of Probate, Hartford, on December 25, 1742, the distribution of Governor Talcott's estate was signed by all children as co-heirs. Among the signatures: 'Nathaniel Hooker and Eunice Hooker' — his daughter Eunice Talcott and her husband Nathaniel Hooker 2d. The Talcott-Hooker convergence was entered into the public record.

narrative_blocks.header: Three of his children each found a separate
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Eunice married Nathaniel Hooker 2d — direct Hooker surname. His son Matthew married Mary Russell, a Hooker descendant through the Pierpont branch. His daughter Abigail married Rev. Daniel Wadsworth; their son Jeremiah later married Mary's own sister Mehitable Russell, also a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Petitioned for his father's estate by primogeniture
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Talcott was the fourth son of Lt.-Col. John Talcott Jr. but the eldest to survive his father. In 1691, at age 21, he petitioned the General Court for his father's estate by right of primogeniture — an assertive legal claim for colonial Connecticut. He was first mentioned in public life in 1711 as a committee member to lay out the town of Coventry.

bio.notes: Widow of [first husband Wakeman] before marrying Gov. Joseph Talcott (T00045)

INST070.name: Hooker and Chaffee
INST070.notes:
One of the largest and most flourishing mercantile houses of New England before the Revolutionary War. Partners: James Hooker (H00348, silent partner), Horace Hooker (H00350, his brother), and John Chaffee (brother of James's third wife Mary Chaffee). Transacted much business for the Colonial Government. All partners were devoted patriots who gave freely of their means for the Colonial cause. After the war, serious embarrassment from heavy French and Spanish spoliations caused failure and dissolution, scattering its members. For full account see Stiles's New History of Windsor. SUPPLY CHAIN NOTE: The firm operated alongside Jeremiah Wadsworth's commissary operation — both Hooker descendants and Talcott descendants provisioning the Continental Army from the same Connecticut River Valley network. See also LM015 (James Hooker House, 118 Palisado Avenue, Windsor).
[former/alt detail] Hooker & Chaffee; Hooker & Chafee
